summary: German-British Baroque composer (1685–1759), born in the same year as J.S. Bach and Domenico Scarlatti. Worked as kapellmeister to the Elector of Hanover before settling in England. Composed prolifically in opera seria, oratorio, and orchestral genres. His oratorio Messiah, with its famous Hallelujah chorus, is his best-known work. Also composed Water Music, Music for the Royal Fireworks, and the coronation anthem Zadok the Priest.

works.name: Messiah
works.description: Handel's most famous work, an oratorio on the life of Jesus Christ with a libretto by Charles Jennens (HWV 56). Premiered in 1742. Divided into three parts; Part II closes with the Hallelujah chorus, during which audiences traditionally stand — a custom attributed to King George II attending a London performance. Mozart later re-orchestrated it with substantial wind additions. Marin Alsop created a gospel-inspired version in 1992. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Contains the 'Hallelujah' chorus (in Part II); audiences traditionally stand

works.name: Water Music
works.description: A set of three orchestral suites (HWV 348–350) written for King George I's barge trip down the River Thames. The first suite is in F major and D minor and may be the first English music to feature the French horn. The second suite (D major, trumpet-heavy) contains the famous Alla Hornpipe movement. The third suite is in G major and is flute-heavy. First recorded in original orchestration by Charles Mackerras in 1959. Wikipedia

examples: a series of suites composed for King George I's barge trip down the Thames

works.name: Music for the Royal Fireworks
works.description: An outdoor suite (HWV 351) composed to celebrate the 1748 Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (end of the War of Austrian Succession). Originally for wind ensemble only (no strings), calling for 24 oboes, 12 bassoons, and 9 trumpets and 9 horns. Later rescored for full orchestra. Movements include 'La Paix' (Largo alla siciliana) and 'La Rejouissance.' The premiere structure (built by Giovanni Servandoni) caught fire. Handel later rescored it for a Foundling Hospital concert. Wikipedia

works.name: Zadok the Priest
works.description: Coronation anthem (HWV 258) commissioned for the coronation of King George II in 1727. Has been performed at every subsequent British coronation. Opens with a long string ritornello of rising arpeggios before the chorus enters in 7-part harmony. Sets text from the first Book of Kings. The second part features a lively 3/4 dance to the lyric 'And all the people rejoiced.' Wikipedia

works.name: Giulio Cesare in Egitto
works.description: Opera seria (HWV 17) premiered in 1724. Depicts Julius Caesar in Egypt. Cleopatra plots to depose her brother Tolomeo and become sole ruler. The title role was premiered by the castrato Senesino. Contains the aria 'Va tacito e nascosto' for Julius Caesar (a da capo aria with a natural horn obbligato, comparing Caesar to a hunter stalking prey), and Cleopatra's aria 'V'adoro pupille' (with a second set of instruments playing offstage as she seduces Caesar). Sesto swears revenge for his father Pompey's murder in 'Svegliatevi nel core.' Wikipedia

works.name: Rinaldo
works.description: Handel's first opera composed for the London stage (1711), based on Tasso's Gerusalemme Liberata. Contains the aria 'Lascia ch'io pianga' sung by the captive Almirena (held by the Saracen king Argante). Also contains what may be the first ever piccolo part in opera history. The melody of a sarabande from Handel's first opera was reused in an aria in this Tasso-inspired work. Wikipedia

works.name: Serse (Xerxes)
works.description: Opera seria (HWV 40, 1738) about the Persian king Xerxes I. Opens with the aria 'Ombra mai fu,' in which Xerxes addresses the shade of a plane tree — the aria is marked larghetto but became known as 'Handel's Largo.' Contains many short ariosos rather than standard da capo arias, which puzzled original listeners. Wikipedia

works.name: Israel in Egypt
works.description: Oratorio (HWV 54) depicting the biblical Plagues of Egypt. Features extreme word painting: dotted rhythms and demisemiquavers in strings represent frogs leaping and flies buzzing in the alto aria 'Their land brought forth frogs'; a rage aria for bass breaks off after three bars as a descending two-octave scale depicts the singer hurling his spear. Handel reused an interlude from this work for his organ concerto 'The Cuckoo and the Nightingale.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Word painting of plagues: dotted rhythms (frogs leaping) and demisemiquavers (flies buzzing) in 'Their land brought forth frogs'

works.name: Solomon
works.description: Oratorio (HWV 67) whose third act opens with the famous 'Arrival of the Queen of Sheba' sinfonia — a sprightly piece for oboes and strings. Also contains the 'Nightingale Chorus' ('May no rash intruder') with two obbligato flutes and violins imitating birds. A widely-excerpted sinfonia scored for two oboes and strings appears in Solomon. The Queen of Sheba performs arias 'Ev'ry sight these eyes behold' and 'Will the sun forget to streak.' Wikipedia

works.name: Judas Maccabaeus
works.description: Oratorio (HWV 63) in which Simon sings 'Arm, arm ye brave' and the chorus sings 'See, the conqu'ring hero comes.' The tune for the chorus was also used in Solomon. Edmond Budry set the lyrics of his Easter hymn 'Thine Be The Glory' to this tune. The final movement proclaims 'Sound the trumpets! Beat the drums!' with lyrics by Thomas Morell. Wikipedia

works.name: The Harmonious Blacksmith
works.description: The nickname for the Air and Variations finale of Handel's Suite No. 5 in E major for harpsichord (HWV 430). Consists of a theme with five variations that increase in speed. The nickname may derive from the repeated 'B' pedal tone in the first variation, likened to a blacksmith's hammer. Percy Grainger was inspired by the theme to write 'Handel in the Strand.' The theme begins with the notes E, G-sharp, F-sharp, high-B. The G minor Passacaglia and D minor Sarabande from Handel's keyboard suites were arranged for violin and viola by Johan Halvorsen. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Finale (Air and Variations) of Harpsichord Suite No. 5 in E major; nickname from repeated B pedal tone like a blacksmith's hammer

works.name: Opus 6 Concerti Grossi
works.description: A set of 12 concerti grossi (HWV 319–330), modeled on Corelli's Op. 6. Two violins and a cello form the concertino. Two of the concertos quote from Handel's Ode for St. Cecilia's Day. The sixth concerto (in G minor) has an E-flat major musette as its central movement. Concerto No. 1 begins with a ten-bar slow introduction, then a fast fugue with violins playing repeated F's in half notes, then quarter notes, then eighth notes. Arnold Schoenberg arranged one of them as the Concerto for String Quartet and Orchestra. Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: George Frideric Handel (1685–1759) was born in the same year as J.S. Bach and Domenico Scarlatti (the 'Class of 1685'). He worked as kapellmeister to the Elector of Hanover before settling in England. He went blind in his last years, with John Christopher Smith serving as his amanuensis. He was a pioneer of the English oratorio, which led him to abandon Italian opera after Messiah's success. His rival was Giovanni Bononcini (they were compared in John Byram's 'Tweedledum and Tweedledee' epigram). His 105-volume collected edition was mostly produced by Friedrich Chrysander in the late 19th century. He met Corelli through concerts organized by Pietro Ottoboni.

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Saul (oratorio, HWV 53): contains a 'Dead March' for the funeral procession of Saul; the chorus 'How dark, O Lord, are Thy decrees.' Jephtha (HWV 70): Handel's last oratorio, written while going blind; contains 'Waft her, angels, through the skies'; Iphis is not sacrificed contrary to the source material. Belshazzar: oratorio featuring the chorus 'See, from his post Euphrates flies.' Acis and Galatea: drawn from Ovid's Metamorphoses, contains the bass solo 'I rage, I melt, I burn.' Semele: oratorio often performed as an opera. Theodora (oratorio). Deidamia: Handel's last Italian opera, in which Deidamia confesses her love for Achilles. Almira: his first surviving Hamburg opera. Rodelinda: opera in which a deposed King of Lombardy sings 'Dove sei, amato bene?' Harp Concerto in B-flat. Fitzwilliam Sonatas for recorder. Opus 4 organ concertos (No. 13 known as 'The Cuckoo and the Nightingale'). Utrecht Te Deum and Jubilate (celebrating 1714 Treaty of Utrecht). Dettingen Te Deum. Ode for St. Cecilia's Day. Funeral anthem 'The Ways of Zion do Mourn.' Psalm 100 setting in D major (no altos).

comprehensive_summary:
George Frideric Handel (1685–1759) was a German-born Baroque composer who spent most of his career in England. He was born in the same year as J.S. Bach and Domenico Scarlatti — a cohort sometimes called the 'Class of 1685.' Before settling in England, he worked as kapellmeister to the Elector of Hanover. He went blind in his final years, with John Christopher Smith serving as his amanuensis. In the late 19th century, his complete works were collected in a 105-volume edition largely produced by Friedrich Chrysander. His rival was the Italian composer Giovanni Bononcini; the two were famously compared in John Byram's epigram that coined the phrase 'Tweedledum and Tweedledee.' He met Arcangelo Corelli through concerts organized by Pietro Ottoboni, and modeled his own Opus 6 set of 12 concerti grossi on Corelli's.

Handel's most famous work is the oratorio Messiah (HWV 56, 1742), with a libretto by Charles Jennens. It is divided into three parts; Part II closes with the Hallelujah chorus, during which audiences traditionally stand — a custom traced to King George II at a London performance. Purely instrumental segments include the opening E minor French overture ('Sinfony') and the shepherd-inspired 'Pifa' (Pastoral Symphony) in 12/8 time, with violins ascending C–D–E–F–G–A over a bass drone. Part I contains the arias 'Comfort ye my people' and 'Ev'ry valley shall be exalted,' while the bass aria 'The trumpet shall sound' features a solo trumpet imitating the voice. Musicologist Rudolf Steglich identified the ascending fourth as a unifying motif throughout. In 1789, Mozart re-orchestrated Messiah with substantial wind additions. In 1992, Marin Alsop created a gospel-inspired version.

His orchestral output includes Water Music (three suites for King George I's barge trip down the Thames) and Music for the Royal Fireworks (commemorating the 1748 Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle, originally for wind ensemble with 24 oboes and 12 bassoons). The Royal Fireworks suite's premiere structure, built by Giovanni Servandoni, caught fire. Both works remain among the most performed pieces of the Baroque repertoire. The Water Music's D-major suite contains the famous Alla Hornpipe movement; the first suite may be the earliest English music to feature the French horn. The Royal Fireworks suite includes 'La Paix' (Largo alla siciliana) and 'La Rejouissance.'

Handel composed extensively in opera seria. His Giulio Cesare in Egitto (1724) stars Julius Caesar (premiered by castrato Senesino), who sings the hunting aria 'Va tacito e nascosto' with a natural horn obbligato; Cleopatra seduces Caesar in 'V'adoro pupille,' with a second orchestra playing offstage; and Sesto swears revenge on Tolomeo in 'Svegliatevi nel core.' His opera Serse opens with 'Ombra mai fu,' in which Xerxes addresses the shade of a plane tree — though marked larghetto, it became known as 'Handel's Largo.' Rinaldo (his first London opera) contains the aria 'Lascia ch'io pianga' for the captive Almirena, and may include the first piccolo part ever written. Other notable operas include Ariodante, Rodelinda, and Deidamia (his last Italian opera).

Among his oratorios, Israel in Egypt features extreme word painting: dotted rhythms depict frogs leaping and demisemiquavers depict flies buzzing in the plagues sequences; a bass rage aria breaks off as a two-octave descending scale depicts spear-hurling. Solomon features the sprightly 'Arrival of the Queen of Sheba' sinfonia for oboes and strings at the opening of Act 3, and the 'Nightingale Chorus' with two obbligato flutes. Judas Maccabaeus includes Simon's 'Arm, arm ye brave' and the chorus 'See, the conqu'ring hero comes.' Saul contains a 'Dead March' depicting a funeral procession. Jephtha was his last oratorio, written while going blind. His coronation anthem Zadok the Priest, written for George II, has been performed at every British coronation since.

Handel's keyboard music includes 'The Harmonious Blacksmith' — the nickname for the Air and Variations finale of his Suite No. 5 in E major, whose repeated B pedal tone was likened to a blacksmith's hammer. The G minor Passacaglia and D minor Sarabande from his keyboard suites were arranged for violin and viola by Johan Halvorsen. His Opus 6 concerti grossi (12 works) modeled on Corelli include a G minor sixth concerto with an E-flat major musette central movement, and one arranged by Arnold Schoenberg as a Concerto for String Quartet. His Opus 4 organ concertos, performed as interludes during oratorio concerts, are considered the earliest chamber organ concertos — an inventive form he pioneered incidentally through oratorio production.

cards.front: Composer: worked as kapellmeister to the Elector of Hanover before settling in England; born in the same year as J.S. Bach and Domenico Scarlatti
Composer: went blind in final years; John Christopher Smith served as his amanuensis
Composer: rival was Giovanni Bononcini; the two were compared in John Byram's epigram that coined 'Tweedledum and Tweedledee'
Composer: abandoned Italian opera after the success of an oratorio containing the line 'And he shall reign for ever and ever'
Composer: Opus 4 organ concertos are the earliest concertos for chamber organ; performed as interludes during oratorio performances at Covent Garden
Oratorio: libretto by Charles Jennens; audiences stand during Part II's closing chorus by tradition begun at a London performance attended by King George II
Oratorio: opening 'Sinfony' is an E minor French overture; includes a 'Pifa' (Pastoral Symphony) in 12/8 inspired by shepherd-bagpipers
Oratorio: Pifa opens with first violins playing ascending C, D, E, F, G, A over a bass drone on C
Oratorio: Part I contains 'Comfort ye my people' and 'Ev'ry valley shall be exalted'; Hallelujah chorus melody is long D, low A, B, A
Oratorio: re-orchestrated by Mozart in 1789 with substantial wind additions; musicologist Rudolf Steglich identified the ascending fourth as its unifying motif
Suite: commissioned for King George I's barge trip down the River Thames; D-major suite contains an Alla Hornpipe movement
Suite: three suites — F major (French horn prominent; possibly the first English music to feature it), D major (trumpet-heavy), G major (flute-heavy)
Suite: commemorates Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle; premiere structure by Giovanni Servandoni caught fire; originally for wind ensemble with 24 oboes and 12 bassoons
Suite: movements 'La Paix' (Largo alla siciliana) and 'La Rejouissance'; called for 9 natural trumpets and 9 natural horns
Anthem: opens with rising string arpeggios before a 7-part chorus enters; performed at every British coronation since 1727; sets text from the first Book of Kings
Opera: Julius Caesar's aria 'Va tacito e nascosto' features a natural horn obbligato and compares him to a hunter; premiered by castrato Senesino
Opera: Cleopatra seduces Caesar in 'V'adoro pupille' with a second orchestra playing offstage; Sesto swears revenge in 'Svegliatevi nel core'
Opera: Saracen king Argante falls in love with captive Almirena, who sings 'Lascia ch'io pianga'; may contain the first piccolo part ever written
Opera: opens with Xerxes addressing the shade of a plane tree in 'Ombra mai fu'; marked larghetto but known as 'Handel's Largo'
Oratorio: depicts Plagues of Egypt; dotted rhythms represent frogs leaping and demisemiquavers represent flies buzzing in 'Their land brought forth frogs'
Oratorio: bass rage aria breaks off mid-section as a two-octave descending scale depicts spear-hurling; interlude reused in 'The Cuckoo and the Nightingale' organ concerto
Oratorio: Act 3 opens with the 'Arrival of the Queen of Sheba' sinfonia for oboes and strings; 'Nightingale Chorus' features two obbligato flutes imitating birds
Oratorio: Simon sings 'Arm, arm ye brave'; chorus sings 'See, the conqu'ring hero comes'; lyrics by Thomas Morell proclaim 'Sound the trumpets! Beat the drums!'
Suite: Air and Variations finale of Harpsichord Suite No. 5 in E major; theme begins E, G-sharp, F-sharp, high B; repeated B pedal tone likened to a blacksmith's hammer
Suite: G minor Passacaglia and D minor Sarabande from two keyboard suites arranged for violin and viola by Johan Halvorsen
cards.back: The Harmonious Blacksmith / Handel keyboard suites

cards.front: Concerto: set of 12 concerti grossi modeled on Corelli's Op. 6; No. 6 in G minor has an E-flat major musette as its central movement; one arranged by Schoenberg as Concerto for String Quartet

recursive_suggestions: Messiah (Handel) — most frequently clued work; Pifa, Hallelujah chorus, and individual arias deserve dedicated analysis
Giulio Cesare in Egitto (Handel) — many specific aria details for Va tacito, V'adoro pupille, Svegliatevi nel core
Water Music (Handel) — distinct movements with historical context
Music for the Royal Fireworks (Handel) — distinct movements La Paix and La Rejouissance with historical context
